abstract: Cyber-physical systems (CPS) serve as the backbone of modern infrastructure, yet their operational safety is frequently jeopardized by technical faults and anomalies. This study investigates the efficacy of open-source Large Language Models (LLMs)—including Mistral 7B, Llama 3.1 (8B), and Gemma 2—in detecting anomalies across two distinct domains: battery management and powertrain systems. We propose a specialized Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework featuring a novel two-step workflow: the models first infer baseline operational rules from normal behavioral data and subsequently apply those rules to identify deviations. Our experiments revealed that while standard prompting was effective for battery data, the more complex powertrain dataset required an optimized approach. By refining the prompt to emphasize rule inference, we achieved a significant boost in diagnostic accuracy. Results demonstrate that Mistral 7B produced F1-scores of up to 0.99, while Llama 3.1 and Gemma 2 achieved perfect 1.0 F1-scores in challenging scenarios. These findings highlight the critical role of prompt engineering and structured rule inference in leveraging LLMs to bolster the resilience of cyber-physical infrastructure.
